english: joyment of the mouth', i. e. an outward, temporary enjoyment or advantage; hence pʻan-pa (phan pa) and pʻan-pa yin-pa (phan pa yin pa), adj., useful: pʻan-pai don (phan pa'i don), a useful thing, valuable possession, frq.; bdag nyon-moṅs-pa-las pʻan-pai don med (bdag nyon mongs pa las phan pa'i don med), after all it is of no use to me in my misery, Dzl.; bslab-bya pʻan-pai tsʻig (bslab bya phan pa'i tshig), a wholesome instructive word, Glr.; pʻan-pai gros (phan pa'i gros), useful advice, Dzl. II. sbst. use, benefit, profit: bstan-pa-la pʻan-pa źig byed-pa (bstan pa la phan pa zhig byed pa), Stg.; pʻan-gnod-med-pa (phan gnod med pa), bringing neither profit nor harm, Mil.; pʻan-pa daṅ bde-ba (phan pa dang bde ba), pʻan-bde (phan bde) happiness and blessing, very frq.; pʻan-°dogs-pa (phan 'dogs pa), pʻan-°dogs byed-pa (phan 'dogs byed pa), to be of use, and adj. profitable, frq.; pʻan-tʻogs (phan thogs), profit; pʻan-tʻogs cʻe (phan thogs che), Thgy., *cʻen-mo* (chen mo), W., very profitable; ... la pʻan gaṅ tʻogs gyis (... la phan gang thogs gyis), render services to ... in every way possible! Mil.; pʻan-grogs (phan grogs) a helping (useful) friend, Pth.; pʻan-gnod (phan gnod), profit and loss, pʻan-bde (phan bde) v. above; pʻan-zas (phan zas), wholesome diet, Med.; pʻan-yon (phan yon), benefit, blessing, as a reward for a meritorious action, frq.; pʻan-(pai) sems (phan (pa'i) sems), benevolence, readiness to help.
I. v. bebs-pa (bebs pa). — II. Sch.: to fall down (?).
1. dry barm (prepared for inst. in Balti, is said to consist of flour, mixed with some ginger and aconite). — 2. lees, yeast (of beer).

I. sbst. interest (of money), W.: *ṅul-la pʻar kal-ce* (ngul la phar kal ce), to impose, demand interest, *cʻal-ce* (chal ce), to pay interest; exchange, agio. II. in later writings and col. for pʻa (pha) II.; also for pʻan-cʻad (phan chad), pʻa-zad (pha zad): farther; pʻar gro-ba (phar gro ba), to go on; pʻar °kyam-pa (phar 'kyam pa), to roam farther and farther, Thgy.; *pʻar-tsam* (phar tsam), C., = pʻar (phar); pʻar °gro tsur °gro-ba-rnams (phar 'gro tshur 'gro ba rnams), people going, travelling, hither and thither; away, off: di-nas pʻar ('di nas phar), away from here; pʻar mi mcʻio (phar mi mchio), I do not go away, Dzl. 220, 6 (Sch. erron. 'to the father'); pʻar bźud (phar bzhud), go away!; ... la pʻar lta-ba (... la phar lta ba), to look (in a certain direction) Mil., away from one's self, as opp. to: raṅ-rig-sems-la tsur lta-ba (rang rig sems la tshur lta ba), to look into one's own heart Mil.; glu pʻar-cig tsur-cig len-pa (glu phar cig tshur cig len pa), alternative song, Mil.; pʻar-slob tsur-slob yin (phar slob tshur slob yin), they are mutually scholars one of the other, Tar.; pʻar ycig lab-na tsur gnyis rgol (phar gcig lab na tshur gnyis rgol), if you say one word 'towards her', she gives you smartly a double charge back, Mil.; pʻar-tsur-la (phar tshur la), W. also = *so-sor* (so sor), in opposite directions; *pʻar-tsur-la cʻe-ce* (phar tshur la che ce) to separate vb. a.; *do-ce* (do ce), to separate vb. n. Comp. pʻar-kʻa (phar kha), Thgy. pʻar-kʻa (phar kha) = pʻa-rol (pha rol), the opposite side (of a valley &.) vulgo frq. — pʻar-ṅos (phar ngos), id., cʻui pʻar-tsur-gyi-lam (chu'i phar tshur gyi lam). — pʻar-pʻyin (phar phyin) abbreviation for pʻa-rol-tu pʻyin-pa (pha rol tu phyin pa) v. pʻa-rol (pha rol), pʻa (pha) II. Mil. — *pʻar-tsam* (phar tsam), C., = pʻa-zad (pha zad). — pʻar-°dzug (phar 'dzug) and tsur-rgol (tshur rgol) prob. = sṅa-rgol (snga rgol) and pʻyi-rgol (phyi rgol). — pʻar-zad (phar zad) = pʻa-zad (pha zad). — pʻar-la (phar la), 1. = pʻar (phar), away, onward, Schr. 2. = pʻa-rol-tu (pha rol tu), na (na), esp. with regard to time: vulgo lo ycig pʻar-la (lo gcig phar la), after one year; W. esp. after the gerund in nas (nas): *zan zos-ne pʻar-la* (zan zos ne phar la), after dinner. — pʻar-lam (phar lam), way or journey thither, Sp. ni f.
I. 1. wild dog (barks, and commits its ravages like the wolf, yet being afraid of man) in Ld. — 2. wolf C., also pʻar-spyaṅ (phar spyang). II. v. °pʻar-ba ('phar ba).
I.? Ld. 1. *pʻal cʻos-se* (phal chos se) (or *te* (te)) *dug* (dug), step aside! make way! — 2. *pʻal-pʻal cʻa-ce* (phal phal cha ce), to feel flattered. II. v. the following.

usual, common; pʻal-pai miṅ (phal pa'i ming), his usual (common) name, Thgr.; pʻal-pa-las pʻags-par bzaṅ-ba (phal pa las phags par bzang ba), a more than ordinary beauty Dzl.; mi (mi) or gaṅ-zag pʻal-pa (gang zag phal pa), common people, Mil.; tsoṅ-pʻal-rnams bor-ro (tshong phal rnams bor ro), they left the common tradespeople behind, Dzl.; pʻal-pai rdzas (phal pa'i rdzas) v. °jal-ba ('jal ba); śiṅ pʻal-rnams (shing phal rnams), common trees, Mil.; snod pʻal-pa (snod phal pa), common vessels, Mil.; pʻal (phal), the common people; pʻal-gyi naṅ-na gnas-pa (phal gyi nang na gnas pa), to live among the people Dzl.; pʻal-gyis rgyab-nas ded pʻal-gyis bskor-te (phal gyis rgyab nas ded phal gyis bskor te), the people running after and crowding round him, Pth.; *pʻal-(pai) skad* (phal (pa'i) skad), 1. W. the language of common life, opp. to *cʻos-skad* (chos skad), book-lan-
